---
title: "Promoting Commits Across Repositories"
date: '2026-03-06'
categories:
  - Version Control
  - Git
description: |
  A guide on how to use git cherry-pick to promote commits across different repositories.
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-block-border-left: true
    layout-nrow: 1
---
    
## Context

This guide walks through how to selectively move commits **from a source repository into a target repository** using `git cherry-pick`. It is designed for the common scenario where two repositories are related — for example, a developer's fork or a feature repo — and you need to bring specific commits across without pulling in unrelated history or triggering a full merge.

```{bash}
[SOURCE REPO]  ──cherry-pick──►  [TARGET REPO]
 (where work lives)                (where it needs to land)
```

**Typical use case:** A teammate pushed work to their own repo or a dev-specific repo. You need to promote their commits — or your own — into a shared or production-facing repo via a dedicated promotion branch.



## How It Works

Cherry-pick lets you take individual commits from any branch or repository and replay them onto your current branch. Unlike `merge` or `rebase`, it does not pull in surrounding history — only the commits you explicitly select.

The workflow has three phases:

1. **Connect** — temporarily register the source repo as a remote inside the target repo
2. **Select** — preview and identify the commits you want to bring over
3. **Promote** — apply those commits to a new branch and push to the target

The source remote is removed at the end, keeping the target repo clean.


## Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| **Source repo** | Where the commits currently live (e.g. a dev fork, teammate's repo) |
| **Target repo** | Where the commits need to land (e.g. the shared or production repo) |
| **Source remote** | A temporary remote pointing to the source repo, added inside the target |
| **Promotion branch** | A new branch in the target repo created specifically to receive the cherry-picked commits |
| **Author filter** | Optional: limit cherry-picked commits to a specific Git author |



## Step-by-Step Instructions

### 1. Start inside the target repository

All commands in this guide run from within your **target** repo — the one you want to promote commits *into*.

```{bash}
cd <path-to-target-repo>
```



### 2. Register the source repository as a temporary remote

Add the source repo as a named remote so Git can read its history. This does not modify the source repo in any way.

```bash
git remote add <source-remote-name> <path-or-url-to-source-repo>
```

**Examples:**

```bash
# From a local path (sibling directory)
git remote add source-dev ../my-source-repo

# From a remote URL
git remote add source-dev https://github.com/org/source-repo.git
```

> Choose a name that makes it obvious this is a temporary, external remote — e.g. `source-dev`, not just `origin`.
> 



### 3. Fetch the source remote and create a promotion branch

Pull down the source repo's refs, then create a new branch in the target repo to receive the commits.

```bash
git fetch <source-remote-name>
git checkout -b <promotion-branch-name>
```

**Branch naming convention:** `promote/<author>-<ticket-id>_<short-description>`

**Example:**

```bash
git fetch source-dev
git checkout -b promote/alice-PROJ-42_add-json-exporter
```

> This branch lives in the **target repo**. It starts from your current HEAD (e.g. `main` or `master` of the target).
> 



### 4. Preview the commits you intend to promote

Before applying anything, confirm exactly which commits will be cherry-picked. The range `<promotion-branch>..<source-remote>/master` means: *"commits that exist in the source but not yet in our promotion branch."*

**Option A — Filter by a specific author (recommended when promoting your own or one person's work):**

```bash
git log --oneline --no-merges \
  --author="<author-name>" \
  --reverse \
  <promotion-branch-name>..<source-remote-name>/master
```

**Option B — Show all authors with commit SHA, name, and subject:**

```bash
git log --oneline --no-merges \
  --pretty=format:'%h %an %s' \
  --reverse \
  <promotion-branch-name>..<source-remote-name>/master
```

**Example output:**

```
a1b2c3d alice Fix null pointer in JSON parser
e4f5a6b alice Add configurable export interval
7c8d9e0 alice Update default schema path
```

> Review this list carefully — it is the exact set of commits that will be applied in the next step.
> 



### 5. Cherry-pick the commits into the promotion branch

Apply the selected commits onto your promotion branch in chronological order. The `-x` flag appends the original commit SHA to each message, preserving a traceable link back to the source.

**With author filter:**

```bash
git rev-list --no-merges \
  --author="<author-name>" \
  --reverse \
  <promotion-branch-name>..<source-remote-name>/master \
  | xargs -n1 git cherry-pick -x
```

**Without author filter (all commits in range):**

```bash
git rev-list --no-merges \
  --reverse \
  <promotion-branch-name>..<source-remote-name>/master \
  | xargs -n1 git cherry-pick -x
```

> If a conflict occurs, Git will pause and tell you which file to resolve. After resolving, run `git cherry-pick --continue`. To abort the whole operation, run `git cherry-pick --abort`.
> 



### 6. Remove the temporary remote and push

Once the commits are applied, clean up the source remote — it is no longer needed. Then push the promotion branch to the target repo's origin.

```bash
git remote remove <source-remote-name>
git push -u origin <promotion-branch-name>
```

> Removing the remote prevents accidental pushes to the source repo and keeps `git remote -v` readable.
> 



## Quick Reference

```
SETUP
  git remote add <source-remote> <source-path-or-url>
  git fetch <source-remote>
  git checkout -b <promotion-branch>

PREVIEW
  git log --oneline --no-merges --author="<author>" --reverse \
    <promotion-branch>..<source-remote>/master

PROMOTE
  git rev-list --no-merges --author="<author>" --reverse \
    <promotion-branch>..<source-remote>/master \
    | xargs -n1 git cherry-pick -x

PUSH
  git remote remove <source-remote>
  git push -u origin <promotion-branch>
```



## Additional Resources

- [Git cherry-pick documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-cherry-pick)
- [Git rev-list documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-rev-list)
- [Pro Git Book – Distributed Workflows](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Distributed-Git-Distributed-Workflows)
